title: How to Screen and Treat
authors: Simon H. S. Pearce, MD, FRCP

items.title: Why immune checkpoint inhibitors cause endocrine adverse effects
items.text: Checkpoint inhibitors block CTLA4, PD-1, or PD-L1 immune brakes, augmenting antitumor immunity but causing immune-mediated endocrinopathies in ~15% with single agents and 20%–30% with combination therapy.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "immune-mediated endocrine dysfunction is observed in approximately 15% of patients treated with a single agent and in 20% to 30% of those treated with a combination of 2 checkpoint inhibitors."

items.title: How thyroid dysfunction prevalence varies by agent
items.text: Destructive thyroiditis is most common with PD-1/PD-L1 blockers (hypothyroidism up to ~8%–8.5%); hypophysitis is most frequent with CTLA4 blockade (~5% with ipilimumab).
items.reference: Table. Prevalence of Endocrinopathies During Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor Therapy: "Ipilimumab ... Hypothyroidism 3.8% ... Pituitary Dysfunction 5.6% ... Nivolumab ... Hypothyroidism 8% ... Pituitary Dysfunction 0.5%"

items.title: Why hypophysitis with adrenal crisis requires immediate hydrocortisone
items.text: Sick hypotensive patient on combination ipilimumab/nivolumab needs serum cortisol and IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat before MRI or cosyntropin testing.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: B) Measure serum cortisol and give intravenous hydrocortisone, 100 mg stat"

items.title: How to manage asymptomatic checkpoint inhibitor thyrotoxicosis
items.text: Mild asymptomatic thyrotoxicosis: repeat TFT in 2 weeks with TRAb; do not cancel immunotherapy or start thionamides; ~50% develop permanent hypothyroidism.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: E) Order repeated thyroid function tests in 2 weeks with TSH-receptor antibodies"

items.title: Why thionamides should not be used in ICI thyroiditis
items.text: Methimazole does not improve immune-mediated thyrotoxicosis and may prolong or worsen rebound hypothyroidism.
items.reference: Case 2: "Starting methimazole (Answer C) is incorrect, as it will not improve thyrotoxicosis in thyroiditis; it will just make any rebound hypothyroidism more prolonged or severe."

items.title: How to recognize checkpoint inhibitor central hypothyroidism
items.text: Declining free T4 with normal or low TSH during CTLA4 therapy suggests hypophysitis—mandatory screening for adrenal insufficiency before levothyroxine.
items.reference: Hypothyroidism: "It is mandatory to screen for and treat hypoadrenalism before starting levothyroxine replacement."

items.title: Who is at highest risk for ipilimumab hypophysitis
items.text: Hypophysitis is more common in older men (3:1) after 3–5 cycles, especially at 10 mg/kg ipilimumab doses.
items.reference: Pituitary Dysfunction: "It is more common in older men (male:female, 3:1) and with higher dosages of ipilimumab (10 mg/kg)."

items.title: Why treatable endocrinopathy should not end checkpoint therapy
items.text: Transformative cancer benefit means endocrine adverse effects should not routinely lead to discontinuation—temporary pause if significantly unwell.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "development of a treatable endocrine adverse effect should not routinely lead to discontinuation of the checkpoint inhibitor therapy."

items.title: How fulminant type 1 diabetes presents on checkpoint inhibitors
items.text: Fulminant type 1 diabetes with ketoacidosis can occur, more often with combination CTLA4/PD-1 blockade (~2% type 1 diabetes).
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Fulminant type 1 diabetes, often with ketoacidosis, and primary adrenal insufficiency can also be observed but are more rare."

items.title: Pitfall: attributing ICI endocrinopathy symptoms to progressive cancer
items.text: Nonspecific weight loss, lethargy, and hypotension from adrenal insufficiency may be misread as tumor progression.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "nonspecific deterioration, including weight loss, poor appetite, lethargy, and hypotension may be misattributed to advancing cancer rather than to a readily treatable endocrinopathy"

items.title: When to treat checkpoint inhibitor hypothyroidism
items.text: Asymptomatic subclinical hypothyroidism with TSH 4–10 may be observed; treat when TSH ≥10 or symptomatic; assume primary hypothyroidism is permanent.
items.reference: Hypothyroidism: "it is not necessary to treat patients with asymptomatic mild/subclinical primary hypothyroidism with serum TSH concentrations in the range of 4 to 10 mIU/L and normal serum free T₄ levels, but treatment is indicated once the TSH concentration rises to 10 mIU/L or higher."

items.title: What endocrine recovery to expect after checkpoint inhibitors
items.text: Recovery is not expected in most instances; secondary adrenal insufficiency is usually permanent though hypothyroidism/hypogonadism may recover in ~50%.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Recovery from endocrine dysfunction is not expected in most instances."

items.question: Melanoma patient on ipilimumab/nivolumab cycle 3 with hypotension, hyponatremia, confusion. Immediate priority?
options: Measure cortisol, aldosterone, and ACTH only
Measure cortisol and give IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat
items.explanation: Acutely ill patient needs empiric IV hydrocortisone while drawing cortisol; detailed testing waits until stable.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: B) Measure serum cortisol and give intravenous hydrocortisone, 100 mg stat"

items.question: Asymptomatic mild thyrotoxicosis before pembrolizumab cycle 4. Best action?
options: Repeat TFT in 2 weeks with TRAb
items.explanation: Mild asymptomatic thyroiditis: monitor; TRAb helps exclude Graves; thionamides and cycle cancellation inappropriate.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: E) Order repeated thyroid function tests in 2 weeks with TSH-receptor antibodies"

items.question: Which agent has the highest pituitary dysfunction rate in the ICI table?
items.explanation: CTLA4 blockade with ipilimumab carries highest hypophysitis rate in the summary table.
items.reference: Table. Prevalence of Endocrinopathies During Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor Therapy: "Ipilimumab ... Pituitary Dysfunction ... 5.6%"

items.question: Destructive thyroiditis causing hypothyroidism is most common with:
items.explanation: Chapter states destructive thyroiditis is typically seen after PD-1/PD-L1 blockers.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Destructive thyroiditis, manifest as either transient hyperthyroidism or permanent hypothyroidism, is the most common endocrine adverse effect, typically seen after use of PD1/PD-L1 blockers."

items.question: Pituitary MRI in checkpoint inhibitor hypophysitis is indicated to:
items.explanation: MRI excludes metastatic pituitary disease; moderately enlarged bright pituitary may be seen.
items.reference: Pituitary Dysfunction: "Affected patients need pituitary MRI whether they present with mass effects or hormonal dysfunction, as other causes of pituitary failure (particularly metastasis) must be excluded."

items.question: In high tumor burden, central hypothyroidism from hypophysitis differs from sick euthyroid syndrome because:
options: Free T4 is usually lower in central hypothyroidism
TSH is always high in central hypothyroidism
Sick euthyroid always needs levothyroxine urgently
items.explanation: Sick euthyroid tends to preserve free T4 relative to central hypothyroidism with low free T4.
items.reference: Hypothyroidism: "serum free T₄ tends to be relatively preserved in sick euthyroid syndrome and is likely to be lower in central hypothyroidism."

items.question: High-dose methylprednisolone in hypophysitis is appropriate for:
options: Isolated ACTH deficiency without mass effect
Optic nerve compression with visual loss
items.explanation: High-dose glucocorticoids for vision-threatening mass effect until vision restored.
items.reference: Pituitary Dysfunction: "Rarely, patients present with optic nerve compression causing visual disturbance, and they should be treated with high-dosage glucocorticoids such as methylprednisolone or dexamethasone until vision is restored."

items.question: Starting methimazole for pembrolizumab-related thyrotoxicosis may:
items.explanation: Thionamides are ineffective in thyroiditis and may worsen hypothyroid phase.
items.reference: Case 2: "it will just make any rebound hypothyroidism more prolonged or severe."

items.question: Combination CTLA4 + PD-1 checkpoint inhibition endocrine toxicity rate is approximately:
items.explanation: Combination therapy doubles or more the endocrine AE rate vs single agent.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "in 20% to 30% of those treated with a combination of 2 checkpoint inhibitors."

items.question: Before levothyroxine for checkpoint inhibitor hypothyroidism, you must:
options: Screen for and treat adrenal insufficiency
items.explanation: Treating hypothyroidism before cortisol replacement risks adrenal crisis.
items.reference: Hypothyroidism: "It is mandatory to screen for and treat hypoadrenalism before starting levothyroxine replacement."

items.question: In Case 2, repeated labs showed progression to TSH 39 with low free T4 requiring:
items.explanation: Destructive thyroiditis progressed to permanent hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine.
items.reference: Case 2: "Levothyroxine, 75 mcg daily, was started."

items.question: Hypophysitis from ipilimumab typically presents after:
items.explanation: Headache and fatigue emerge after several cycles.
items.reference: Pituitary Dysfunction: "Headache and fatigue are the earliest symptoms and are typically found after 3 to 5 cycles (9-16 weeks)."

items.question: Primary adrenal insufficiency with checkpoint inhibitors is:
options: Most common with single PD-1 agents
Rare with single agents; higher with combination CTLA4/PD-1
items.explanation: Primary adrenal insufficiency is rare on monotherapy but more frequent with combination blockade.
items.reference: Primary Adrenal Insufficiency: "Primary adrenal insufficiency seems to be rare with single-agent checkpoint inhibition, but combination CTLA4/PD-1 blockade therapy"

items.statement: Thyroid dysfunction is the most common endocrine adverse effect of checkpoint inhibitors.
items.explanation: Destructive thyroiditis predominates, especially with PD-1/PD-L1 agents.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Destructive thyroiditis ... is the most common endocrine adverse effect"

items.statement: Endocrine adverse effects should routinely lead to permanent checkpoint inhibitor discontinuation.
items.explanation: Treat endocrinopathy and continue therapy when possible; pause if very unwell.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "should not routinely lead to discontinuation of the checkpoint inhibitor therapy."

items.statement: Acutely ill suspected hypophysitis patient should receive IV hydrocortisone 100 mg immediately.
items.explanation: Empiric steroids while measuring cortisol in sick patient per Case 1.
items.reference: Case 1: "give intravenous hydrocortisone, 100 mg stat (Answer B) is correct because he is sick"

items.statement: Methimazole improves immune checkpoint inhibitor-mediated thyrotoxicosis.
items.explanation: Thyroiditis thyrotoxicosis is self-limited; thionamides are ineffective.
items.reference: Case 2: "Starting methimazole (Answer C) is incorrect"

items.statement: Ipilimumab causes hypophysitis in approximately 5% of patients.
items.explanation: Table lists pituitary dysfunction 5.6% for ipilimumab.
items.reference: Table. Prevalence of Endocrinopathies During Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor Therapy: "Ipilimumab ... Pituitary Dysfunction ... 5.6%"

items.statement: Primary hypothyroidism after checkpoint inhibitor thyroiditis should be assumed permanent.
items.explanation: Long-term levothyroxine usually required.
items.reference: Hypothyroidism: "Primary hypothyroidism should be assumed to be permanent."

items.statement: Mild asymptomatic thyrotoxicosis warrants cancelling the next checkpoint inhibitor cycle.
items.explanation: Case 2: not ill enough to interrupt life-prolonging therapy.
items.reference: Case 2: "Cancelling this cycle of pembrolizumab (Answer A) is wrong because she is not ill enough"

items.statement: Most checkpoint inhibitor endocrinopathies fully recover without replacement therapy.
items.explanation: Recovery not expected in most instances.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Recovery from endocrine dysfunction is not expected in most instances."

items.assertion: Assertion: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg is priority in sick suspected ICI adrenal crisis patient.
items.reason: Reason: Because cosyntropin testing must always precede any glucocorticoid.
items.explanation: Assertion true when ill; reason false—treat first, test when stable.
items.reference: Case 1: "he is sick and should be treated immediately with parenteral hydrocortisone"

items.assertion: Assertion: Asymptomatic mild ICI thyrotoxicosis can be monitored with repeat TFT in 2 weeks.
items.reason: Reason: Because thionamides are first-line for immune-mediated thyroiditis.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—thionamides contraindicated.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: E) Order repeated thyroid function tests in 2 weeks with TSH-receptor antibodies"

items.assertion: Assertion: CTLA4 blockade carries higher hypophysitis risk than PD-1 monotherapy.
items.reason: Reason: Because ipilimumab pituitary dysfunction rate exceeds nivolumab in prevalence table.
items.explanation: Both true—5.6% vs 0.5% in table.
items.reference: Table. Prevalence of Endocrinopathies During Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor Therapy: "Ipilimumab ... Pituitary Dysfunction ... 5.6% ... Nivolumab ... 0.5%"

items.assertion: Assertion: Adrenal insufficiency must be treated before levothyroxine in ICI hypophysitis.
items.reason: Reason: Because levothyroxine can precipitate adrenal crisis if cortisol is deficient.
items.explanation: Both true—standard hypophysitis management.
items.reference: Hypothyroidism: "It is mandatory to screen for and treat hypoadrenalism before starting levothyroxine replacement."

items.assertion: Assertion: Combination checkpoint inhibition increases endocrine adverse events.
items.reason: Reason: Because combination therapy reduces immune activation.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—combination increases immune toxicity.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "in 20% to 30% of those treated with a combination of 2 checkpoint inhibitors."

items.assertion: Assertion: ICI adrenal insufficiency may mimic progressive malignancy.
items.reason: Reason: Because lethargy and weight loss never occur in cancer patients.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason absurd—symptoms overlap with cancer progression.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "may be misattributed to advancing cancer rather than to a readily treatable endocrinopathy"

items.assertion: Assertion: Secondary adrenal insufficiency from ICI hypophysitis is usually permanent.
items.reason: Reason: Because all pituitary axes always recover within 2 weeks.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—recovery rare for ACTH deficiency.
items.reference: Pituitary Dysfunction: "Secondary adrenal insufficiency should be assumed to be permanent, although rare cases of recovery have been documented."

items.assertion: Assertion: Approximately 50% of ICI thyrotoxicosis patients develop permanent hypothyroidism.
items.reason: Reason: Because thionamides prevent hypothyroid progression.
items.explanation: Assertion true per Case 2; reason false—thionamides worsen hypothyroid phase.
items.reference: Case 2: "permanent hypothyroidism occurs in approximately 50% of cases."
